en.extract:
The Mindoro cuckooshrike (Coracina mindorensis) is a passerine bird in the family Campephagidae that is endemic to the islands of Mindoro and Tablas in the Philippines. The species was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the bar-bellied cuckooshrike.


Description and taxonomy
This species is described as a large cuckooshrike with pale gray eyes and its plummage is almost uniform gray with a black mask reaching the forehead and lores. This species exhibits sexual dimorphism as females have some slight barring on the underwing.  
The Mindoro cuckooshrike was formally described in 1890 by the American ornithologist Joseph Beal Steere based on a specimen collected on the island of Mindoro in the Philippines. He coined the binomial name Artamides mindorensis. It was formerly treated as a subspecies of the bar-bellied cuckooshrike (Coracina striata) but was promoted to species status based on the significant differences in vocalizations and plumage. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Ecology and behavior
This cuckooshrike usually lives in small groups or in mixed-species foraging flocks with other cuckooshrikes such as the Black-bibbed cicadabird. 
As a newly split species, not much is known about this species itself but based on information about the Bar-bellied cuckooshrike it is also presumed to mainly eats insects, including caterpillars, mantises, and dragonflies, and figs. Breeds from April to May.  Nest is described as a shallow cup of moss, leaves, roots and mud around 20 meters above the ground. Lays 2 pale gray eggs. Only the female has been observed feeding the chicks.


Habitat and conservation status
Its natural habitat is tropical moist lownland and montane forests up to 2,000 meters above sea level. It is typically found in the canopy.
This species has yet to be assessed by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. However, this species is likely declining and possibly threatened as a large majority of Mindoro and Romblon endemic birds are threatened species.  This species' main threat is habitat loss with wholesale clearance of forest habitats as a result of legal and illegal logging, and conversion into farmlands through Slash-and-burn and other methods.The species does not occur at high density even within the little remaining forest cover on Tablas. On Mindoro,  by 1988, extensive deforestation had reduced forest cover to a mere 120 km2, most of which are in the highlands. The lowland forest that does remain is highly fragmented. Slash-and-burn cultivation, occasional selective logging and rattan collection threaten the forest fragments that still support the species. Dynamite blasting for marble is an additional threat to forest at Puerto Galera
Occurs in a few protected areas in the Dubduban Watershed in Tablas, Mount Halcon, Mounts Iglit–Baco Natural Park, Mount Calavite and the Siburan Forest Reserve but actual protection and enforcement from illegal logging and hunting are lax.

The '''Mindoro cuckooshrike''' ('''''Coracina mindorensis''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Campephagidae]] that is endemic to the islands of [[Mindoro]] and [[Tablas Island|Tablas]] in the Philippines. The species was formerly considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[bar-bellied cuckooshrike]].

== Description and taxonomy ==
This species is described as a large cuckooshrike with pale gray eyes and its plummage is almost uniform gray with a black mask reaching the forehead and lores. This species exhibits sexual dimorphism as females have some slight barring on the underwing. <ref>{{Cite book |last=Allen |first=Desmond |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/1286814135 |title=Birds of the Philippines |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=2020 |isbn=978-84-16728-32-9 |pages=240–243 |oclc=1286814135}}</ref> 

The Mindoro cuckooshrike was [[species description|formally described]] in 1890 by the American ornithologist [[Joseph Beal Steere]] based on a specimen collected on the island of [[Mindoro]] in the Philippines. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Artamides mindorensis''.<ref>{{cite book |last=Steere |first=Joseph Beal |author-link=Joseph Beal Steere |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40202749 |title=A List of the Birds and Mammals collected by the Steere Expedition to the Philippines : with Localities, and with Brief Preliminary Descriptions of Supposed New Species |date=1890 |publisher=Courier Office, Printers |location=Ann Arbor, Michigan |page=14}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480852 |title=Check-List of Birds of the World |publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology |year=1960 |editor1-last=Mayr |editor1-first=Ernst |editor1-link=Ernst Mayr |volume=9 |place=Cambridge, Massachusetts |page=177 |editor2-last=Greenway |editor2-first=James C. Jr}}</ref> It was formerly treated as a [[subspecies]] of the [[bar-bellied cuckooshrike]] (''Coracina striata'') but was promoted to species status based on the significant differences in vocalizations and plumage. The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name="ioc">{{cite web |date=August 2024 |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=Frank |editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |editor3-last=Rasmussen |editor3-first=Pamela |editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen |title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ |access-date=1 October 2024 |work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union}}</ref>

== Ecology and behavior ==
This cuckooshrike usually lives in small groups or in [[Mixed-species foraging flock|mixed-species foraging flocks]] with other cuckooshrikes such as the [[Black-bibbed cicadabird]]. 

As a newly split species, not much is known about this species itself but based on information about the [[Bar-bellied cuckooshrike]] it is also presumed to mainly eats insects, including [[Caterpillar|caterpillars]], [[mantises]], and [[dragonflies]], and [[figs]]. Breeds from April to May.  Nest is described as a shallow cup of moss, leaves, roots and mud around 20 meters above the ground. Lays 2 pale gray eggs. Only the female has been observed feeding the chicks.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Taylor |first1=Barry |last2=del Hoyo |first2=Josep |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M. |last4=Collar |first4=Nigel |date=2020 |title=Bar-bellied Cuckooshrike (Coracina striata), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/babcus1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.babcus1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
Its natural [[habitat]] is tropical moist lownland and montane [[Forest|forests]] up to 2,000 meters above sea level. It is typically found in the canopy.

This species has yet to be assessed by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]]. However, this species is likely declining and possibly threatened as a large majority of Mindoro and Romblon endemic birds are threatened species.  This species' main threat is [[habitat loss]] with wholesale clearance of forest habitats as a result of legal and [[illegal logging]], and conversion into farmlands through [[Slash-and-burn]] and other methods.The species does not occur at high density even within the little remaining forest cover on Tablas. On Mindoro,  by 1988, extensive deforestation had reduced forest cover to a mere 120 km<sup>2</sup>, most of which are in the highlands. The lowland forest that does remain is highly fragmented. Slash-and-burn cultivation, occasional selective logging and rattan collection threaten the forest fragments that still support the species. [[Dynamite]] blasting for marble is an additional threat to forest at [[Puerto Galera]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=International) |first=BirdLife International (BirdLife |date=2016-10-01 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Prioniturus mindorensis |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en |access-date=2021-09-13 |website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref>

Occurs in a few protected areas in the Dubduban Watershed in Tablas, [[Mount Halcon]], [[Mounts Iglit–Baco Natural Park]], [[Mount Calavite]] and the Siburan Forest Reserve but actual protection and enforcement from [[illegal logging]] and hunting are lax.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Bar-bellied Cuckooshrike - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/babcus1 |access-date=2024-10-08 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref>
